en.extract:
The western bowerbird (Chlamydera guttata) is a species of bird in the family Ptilonorhynchidae. The species is a common endemic of Australia. It has a disjunct distribution, occurring in Central Australia and the Pilbara region of Western Australia.


Taxonomy
There are two subspecies, the nominate Chlamydera guttata guttata, which occupies most of its range, and C. guttata cateri, which occurs only the North West Cape in Western Australia. The species was first collected in 1861 by the explorer Francis Gregory, who gave the specimen to John Gould. The specific name guttata means spotted, referring to its plumage. The species is similar to Chlamydera maculata, the spotted bowerbird, and has often been treated as a subspecies of it.


Description
The western bowerbird is smaller than the other bowerbird it shares its range with, the great bowerbird. It measures 24–28 cm (9.4–11.0 in) in length and weighs 120–150 g (4.2–5.3 oz). Both sexes are similar in size and dimensions, except that the tail of the female is slightly longer. The plumage is brown with reddish or buff spots over the throat, chest, neck, back, head and wings, with a pink erectile crest on the nape. The tail is brown and the undersides are buff. The plumage of the female is similar to the male but more spotted on the throat.


Diet
The western bowerbird feeds on fruits, particularly those of the rock fig (Ficus platypoda), sandalwood, snake gourd (Trichosanthes cucumerina) and mistletoes. They also enter farms to feed on cultivated fruits. Other items taken in the diet include nectar, flowers, insects (e.g. ants, moths, beetles and grasshoppers), and spiders.  They need to drink regularly and are rarely found far from water.


Behaviour

Like most bowerbirds the western bowerbird is polygamous, with a male mating with several females over a breeding season and the female undertaking all nesting, incubation and chick-rearing responsibilities. As is typical in the family the male constructs an elaborate bower with which to attract females. Western bowerbird bowers are paired avenues of sticks, 20–25 cm (7.9–9.8 in) tall and 25–35 cm (9.8–13.8 in) long, placed on top of a platform of sticks. The bower is decorated with green and white objects, including berries, fruits, shells, pebbles and bones. Man-made objects may also be used for decoration, including glass, gun casings and metal objects. Each bower may be used for a number of years; when a new bower is constructed, material from the older bower may be reused. The male advertises his bower with calls; when the female arrives to inspect he will perform ritualised dances, fanning his tail, jumping and wing flicking, as well as further calling. Decorations will also be held in the bill and shaken vigorously, and the pink crest on the neck will be erected.
The female constructs the nest, a shallow cup of small twigs nested on a larger platform of sticks, around 2–6 m (6.6–19.7 ft) up a tree or shrub, often in a clump of mistletoe. The clutch is usually one or two eggs, which are green to buff, glossy and covered in dark scribble patterns. The incubation period is unknown.


References

The '''western bowerbird''' ('''''Chlamydera guttata''''') is a species of [[bird]] in the [[Family (taxonomy)|family]] [[Ptilonorhynchidae]]. The species is a common [[endemism|endemic]] of [[Australia]]. It has a disjunct [[range (biology)|distribution]], occurring in [[Central Australia]] and the [[Pilbara]] region of [[Western Australia]].<ref name ="Rowland">{{cite book|last=Rowland|first=Peter|title=Bowerbirds|publisher=CSIRO Publishing|location=Collingwood|year=2008|series=Australian Natural History Series|pages=85–91|isbn=978-0-643-09420-8}}</ref>

==Taxonomy==
There are two subspecies, the nominate ''Chlamydera guttata guttata'', which occupies most of its range, and ''C. guttata cateri'', which occurs only the [[North West Cape]] in Western Australia. The species was first [[bird collections|collected]] in 1861 by the explorer [[Francis Gregory]], who gave the specimen to [[John Gould]]. The specific name ''guttata'' means spotted, referring to its [[plumage]]. The species is similar to ''[[Chlamydera maculata]]'', the spotted bowerbird, and has often been treated as a subspecies of it.<ref name ="Rowland"/>

<!-- [[File:Chlamydera guttata.jpg|thumb|left|230px|Captive western bowerbird in [[Alice Springs]]]]
 -->
==Description==
The western bowerbird is smaller than the other bowerbird it shares its range with, the [[great bowerbird]]. It measures {{Cvt|24-28|cm}} in length and weighs {{Cvt|120-150|g}}. Both sexes are similar in size and dimensions, except that the tail of the female is slightly longer.<ref name ="Rowland"/> The [[plumage]] is brown with reddish or buff spots over the throat, chest, neck, back, head and wings, with a pink erectile [[Crest (feathers)|crest]] on the nape.<ref>{{cite book|last=Pizzey|first=Graham|author2=Frank Knight |title=The Field Guide to the Birds of Australia|publisher=Harper Collins|location=Sydney|year=2003|page=480|isbn=0-207-19821-7}}</ref> The tail is brown and the undersides are buff. The plumage of the female is similar to the male but more spotted on the throat.<ref name ="Rowland"/>

==Diet==
The western bowerbird feeds on fruits, particularly those of the rock fig (''[[Ficus platypoda]]''), [[sandalwood]], snake gourd (''[[Trichosanthes cucumerina]]'') and [[mistletoe]]s. They also enter farms to feed on cultivated fruits. Other items taken in the diet include [[nectar]], flowers, insects (e.g. ants, moths, beetles and grasshoppers), and spiders.  They need to drink regularly and are rarely found far from water.<ref name ="Rowland"/>

==Behaviour==
[[File:Chlamydera guttata 03 - Christopher Watson.jpg|thumb|Western bowerbirds build bowers similar to the picture above.]]
Like most bowerbirds the western bowerbird is [[polygamy|polygamous]], with a male mating with several females over a breeding season and the female undertaking all nesting, [[avian incubation|incubation]] and chick-rearing responsibilities. As is typical in the family the male constructs an elaborate [[Bowerbird#Mating behaviour|bower]] with which to attract females. Western bowerbird bowers are paired avenues of sticks, {{Cvt|20-25|cm}} tall and {{Cvt|25-35|cm}} long, placed on top of a platform of sticks. The bower is decorated with green and white objects, including berries, fruits, shells, pebbles and bones. Man-made objects may also be used for decoration, including glass, gun casings and metal objects.<ref name ="Rowland"/> Each bower may be used for a number of years; when a new bower is constructed, material from the older bower may be reused. The male advertises his bower with [[bird vocalizations|calls]]; when the female arrives to inspect he will perform ritualised dances, fanning his tail, jumping and wing flicking, as well as further calling. Decorations will also be held in the bill and shaken vigorously, and the pink crest on the neck will be erected.<ref name ="Rowland"/>

The female constructs the [[bird nest|nest]], a shallow cup of small twigs nested on a larger platform of sticks, around {{Cvt|2-6|m|ft}} up a tree or shrub, often in a clump of [[mistletoe]]. The [[clutch (eggs)|clutch]] is usually one or two eggs, which are green to buff, glossy and covered in dark scribble patterns. The incubation period is unknown.<ref name ="Rowland"/>

zh.extract:
西大亭鸟（台湾作西部大園丁鳥，学名：Chlamydera guttata），是雀形目园丁鸟科的一种鸟类。
西大亭鸟体重约135.98克，翼长约141.6毫米，嘴峰长约27.1毫米，喙宽度约6.9毫米，喙厚度约8.9毫米，跗蹠长约39.3毫米，尾长约92.6毫米。西大亭鸟在其分布区内为留鸟，其栖息在半开放的灌木丛中，食性为草食性。


亚种
C. g. guttata：分布于西澳大利亚州的西北角地区。
C. g. carteri：分布于西澳大利亚州的皮尔布拉至澳大利亚中部。
